title: Work & the Work-Energy Theorem
summary: Start from zero: what work really is, when a force does none, positive vs negative work, forces at an angle, and how net work sets an object's speed.

steps.title: What counts as work?
steps.body:
Here's the very first surprise: in physics, **work is not the same as effort**. You can strain every muscle holding a heavy box over your head and do **zero** work on it.

**Work** is energy transferred to an object by a force — but *only* when the object actually **moves in the direction of that force**.

Tap through the four situations below. Before you read each verdict, predict: is work being done on the box?

steps.title: Putting a number on work
steps.body:
When a force pushes in the **same direction** something moves, work is simply force times distance:

**F** is the force in newtons (N). **d** is the distance it moves in meters (m). Multiply them and you get work in **joules (J)** — where 1 joule = 1 newton pushing through 1 meter (1 J = 1 N·m).

One condition to remember: this clean W = F·d holds when the force is **constant** — a steady, continuous push that doesn't change as the object moves. (If the force varied, you'd use its average, or the area under a force-vs-distance graph. Every force in this lesson is constant.)

Play with it below: drag the **flag** to change how far the box moves, and pull the **back of the blue arrow** to change how hard you push. Watch the work bar respond to *both*.

steps.prompt: Warm-up. A mover pushes a crate with a steady **80 N**, and it slides **3.0 m** in the same direction across a smooth floor. How much work does the mover do on the crate?
hints: Force and motion point the same way, so just multiply them.
steps.solution:
The push and the motion are in the same direction, so work is just force times distance: W = F_A·d.

W = 80 × 3.0 = **240 J**.

steps.prompt: You push a box with a **constant 10 N** force (a steady, continuous push). Drag the **distance** and watch the work change.
steps.task: Drag the distance to **double** it — from 2 m to 4 m. Then read the two bars.
steps.solution: Work is force times distance: W = F·d. The force stays a steady 10 N, so only the distance changes. Going from 2 m to 4 m doubles the distance, and since work is directly proportional to distance, the work doubles too — from 20 J to **40 J (2×)**.

steps.title: When is there NO work?
steps.body:
This is where almost everyone slips. A force can be **huge** and still do **zero** work:

• **Holding** a heavy box perfectly still — your arms ache, but the box doesn't move. No distance means no work (d = 0).
• **Carrying** a box at a steady height across a room — you push **up** to support it, but it moves **sideways**. The force and the motion are perpendicular, so *that* force does no work on the box.

The rule that saves you every time: work only happens when there's motion **in the direction of the force**.

steps.title: Positive, negative, and zero work
steps.body:
Here's the single most important thing work does: **it changes an object's kinetic energy — its speed.** The *sign* of the work tells you which way:

• **Positive work:** the force runs *with* the motion, so energy goes into the object and it **speeds up** (KE increases).
• **Negative work:** the force runs *against* the motion, so energy is pulled out and it **slows down** (KE decreases). Friction is the classic example — it always drags backward.
• **Zero work:** either the force or the distance is zero, *or* the force is perpendicular to the motion. No energy is transferred, so the speed doesn't change.

That's the heart of it: **positive work speeds things up, negative work slows them down.** Get the signs right and the rest of mechanics falls into place.

steps.title: When the push is at an angle
steps.body:
Real forces aren't always lined up with the motion. Only the part of the force **along** the direction of travel does work — the rest just pushes into (or lifts off) the surface.

Here's the part that trips people up: pull straight up as hard as you want, but if the box only slides **sideways**, that upward pull does **zero work**. Work needs motion *in the direction of the force* — no vertical distance means no vertical work, no matter how big the force.

So for a force at an angle **θ** to the motion, only the component F·cos θ counts. And its *direction* sets the sign: swing the rope **forward** (θ < 90°) and that part runs with the motion → **positive** work, speeding the box up. Swing it **backward** (θ > 90°) and it runs against the motion → **negative** work, slowing the box down — even though the box keeps sliding the same way. Drag the rope all the way around to see it:

steps.prompt: A child drags a wagon **12 m** along flat ground, pulling the handle with **40 N** at **35° above horizontal**. How much work does the rope do on the wagon? (cos 35° ≈ 0.819)
hints: Only the horizontal part of the pull moves the wagon — that's F_A·cos θ.
Find the component along the ground first: 40 × cos 35° ≈ 32.8 N. Then multiply by the 12 m.
steps.solution:
Only the component of the force along the motion does work, so W = F_A·d·cos θ.

The horizontal component of the pull is 40 × cos 35° ≈ 32.8 N. Over 12 m that's W = 32.8 × 12 ≈ **393 J**. (The vertical component does no work — the wagon doesn't move up.)

steps.title: Many forces? Use the NET work
steps.body:
Most real situations have **several** forces doing work at once. Add up their works — keeping the signs from the last screen — and you get the **net work**.

For a crate you push across a real floor, that's your applied push **F_A** (positive) plus friction **F_f** (negative):

steps.title: Where the work goes: the work-energy theorem
steps.body:
First, clear a classic misconception: a moving object does **not** need a constant forward force to keep moving — that's just inertia (Newton's first law). What a net force actually does is **change** the motion.

The work-energy theorem makes that precise: the **net work** done on an object equals its **change in kinetic energy**. Positive net work speeds it up, negative net work slows it down. It's one of the most useful tools in all of mechanics.

Launch the block below under a steady push: the net work done (F·d) and the kinetic energy it gains climb together and stay equal the whole way. Then try a heavier block — the same net work always becomes the same kinetic energy (it just ends up slower).

steps.prompt: Quick theorem check. You do **50 J** of net work on a **2.0 kg** cart that starts from rest on a frictionless track. What is the cart's change in kinetic energy?
hints: Read the work-energy theorem literally — it connects net work to ΔKE directly.
ΔKE equals W_net. No need to compute ½mv² here.
steps.solution:
The work-energy theorem says W_net = ΔKE. If 50 J of net work is done, the kinetic energy changes by exactly **50 J** — no ½mv² calculation needed.

(The 2.0 kg mass is a distractor here. It would only matter if the question asked for the cart's *speed*.)

steps.prompt:
**Setup:** You push a **4.0 kg** crate **5.0 m** across a level floor with a steady horizontal **30 N**. Friction pushes back with **12 N**, and the crate starts from rest.

**Part A —** What is the *net* work done on the crate?
hints: Friction does negative work, so the net force along the floor is F_A − F_f.
Net force = 30 − 12 = 18 N. Net work = that net force × the 5.0 m.
steps.solution:
Your push does positive work and friction does negative work, so the net force along the motion is F_A − F_f = 30 − 12 = 18 N.

W_net = (F_A − F_f)·d = 18 × 5.0 = **90 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** Using the work-energy theorem, how fast is the **4.0 kg** crate moving after that 5.0 m? (It started from rest, and W_net = 90 J.)
hints: From rest, all the net work becomes kinetic energy: W_net = ½mv².
steps.solution:
From rest, the work-energy theorem gives W_net = ΔKE = ½mv².

Solve for v: v = √(2·W_net / m) = √(2·90 / 4) = √45 ≈ **6.7 m/s**.

steps.prompt: **Challenge — put it all together.** A **6.0 kg** sled starts from rest and is pulled **8.0 m** across flat snow by a rope at **25 N**, angled **30° above horizontal**. Friction does **−60 J** of work along the way. How fast is the sled moving after 8.0 m? (cos 30° ≈ 0.866)
hints: Two forces do work: the rope (use F_A·d·cos θ) and friction (already given as −60 J). Add them for the net work.
Rope work = 25 × 8.0 × cos 30° ≈ 173 J. Net work = 173 − 60 = 113 J.
Then from rest, W_net = ½mv² → v = √(2·W_net / m) = √(2·113 / 6).
steps.solution:
Find each work, then combine. The rope's work uses only the component along the motion: W_rope = F_A·d·cos θ = 25 × 8.0 × 0.866 ≈ 173 J.

Friction already does −60 J, so the net work is W_net = 173 − 60 = 113 J.

Starting from rest, the work-energy theorem gives W_net = ½mv², so v = √(2·W_net / m) = √(2·113 / 6) = √37.7 ≈ **6.1 m/s**.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You built work from the ground up: what it is, when a force does none, how sign works, forces at an angle, and how net work sets an object's speed through the work-energy theorem — all the way to a multi-force challenge.
